content: A vessel is filled with a gas at a pressure of 76 cm of mercury at a certain temperature. The mass of gas is increased by $50 \%$ introducing more gas in the vessel at the same temperature. Now the resultant pressure of the gas is
explanation:
According to kinetic theory of gases

$$ p V=\frac{1}{3} M V_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 $$

at constant temperature $V_{\text {rms }}^2$ is constant, as $V$ is also constant, so $p \propto M$

When the mass of gas increases by $50 \%$ pressure also increases by 50\%.

So, final pressure $=76+\frac{50}{100} \times 76=114 \mathrm{~cm}$ of Hg .

content: A cylinder of radius $R$ made of a material of thermal conductivity $k_1$ is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius $R$ and outer radius $2 R$ made of a material of thermal conductivity $k_2$. The two ends of a combined system are maintained at two different temperature. There is no loss of heat across the cylindrical surface and the system is in steady state. The effective thermal conductivity of the system is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since, two cylinders are in parallel combination }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{1}{R_e}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{k_e A_e}{l}=\frac{k_1 A_1}{l}+\frac{k_2 A_2}{l} \\ \Rightarrow & k_e \pi(2 R)^2=k_1 \pi(R)^2+k_2 \pi\left[(2 R)^2-(R)^2\right] \\ \Rightarrow & 4 k_e=k_1+3 k_2 \\ \therefore & k_e=\frac{k_1+3 k_2}{4} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: In a certain process, 400 cal of heat is supplied to a system and at the same time 105 J of mechanical work was done on the system. The increase in its internal energy is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned}Here, \,\, d Q & =400 \mathrm{cal}, d W=-105 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =-\frac{105}{4.2} \mathrm{cal}=-25 \mathrm{cal} ; d U=? \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Now, $d U=d Q-d W$

$$ d U=400-(-25)=425 \mathrm{cal} $$

Note $d W$ is negative because work is done on the system.

content: Two rods $P$ and $Q$ have equal lengths. Their thermal conductivities are $K_1$ and $K_2$ and cross-sectional areas are $A_1$ and $A_2$. When the temperature at ends of each rod are $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively, the rate of flow of heat through $P$ and $Q$ will be equal, if
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { As, }\left(\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}\right)_P=\left(\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}\right)_Q \\ & \Rightarrow K_1 A_1 \frac{\left(T_1-T_2\right)}{l}=K_2 A_2 \frac{\left(T_1-T_2\right)}{l} \\ & \text { or } \quad K_1 A_1=K_2 A_2 \text { or } \frac{A_1}{A_2}=\frac{K_2}{K_1} \end{aligned} $$

content: In the given cycle $ A B C D A $, the heat required for an ideal monoatomic gas will be
explanation:
In the cycle $ A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow A $, heat is added to the system during the processes $ D \rightarrow A $ (at constant volume) and $ A \rightarrow B $ (at constant pressure).

Calculating the Heat Added

Heat for Process $ D \rightarrow A $ (Constant Volume):

For an ideal monoatomic gas, the molar heat capacity at constant volume $ C_V $ is given by:

$ C_V = \frac{3}{2} R $

The heat added during this process is:

$ Q_{DA} = n C_V (\Delta T)_{DA} = \frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T $

Heat for Process $ A \rightarrow B $ (Constant Pressure):

The molar heat capacity at constant pressure $ C_p $ is given by:

$ C_p = \frac{5}{2} R $

The heat added during this process is:

$ Q_{AB} = n C_p (\Delta T)_{AB} = \frac{5}{2} n R \Delta T $

Total Heat Supplied

The total heat supplied in the cycle is the sum of the heat added during these two processes:

$ Q = Q_{DA} + Q_{AB} $

Substitute the expressions for $ Q_{DA} $ and $ Q_{AB} $:

$ Q = \frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T + \frac{5}{2} n R \Delta T $

Given $ n R \Delta T = p \Delta V $, and substituting it:

$ Q = \frac{3}{2}(p_0 V_0) + \frac{5}{2}(p_0 V_0) $

This simplifies to:

$ Q = \frac{13}{2} p_0 V_0 $

content: In a mixture of gases, the average number of degree of freedoms per molecule is 6 . The rms speed of the molecule of the gas is $ c $, then the velocity of sound in the gas is
explanation:
In a mixture of gases where the average number of degrees of freedom per molecule is 6, the velocity of sound in the gas can be determined using the following relationships:

The root mean square (rms) speed $ v_{\mathrm{rms}} $ is given by:

$ v_{\mathrm{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} $

The velocity of sound $ v_{\text{sound}} $ is given by:

$ v_{\text{sound}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}} $

The ratio of the velocity of sound to the rms speed is:

$ \frac{v_{\text{sound}}}{v_{\mathrm{rms}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{3}} $

Given that the degrees of freedom $ f = 6 $, we calculate the adiabatic index $ \gamma $ as follows:

$ \gamma = 1 + \frac{2}{f} = 1 + \frac{2}{6} = \frac{4}{3} $

Substituting $ \gamma = \frac{4}{3} $ into the equation for the velocity of sound:

$ v_{\text{sound}} = \sqrt{\frac{4/3}{3}} \, v_{\mathrm{rms}} = \frac{2}{3} v_{\mathrm{rms}} $

Therefore, the velocity of sound in the gas is:

$ \frac{2c}{3} $

content: A vessel containing 1 mole of $$\mathrm{O}_2$$ gas (molar mass 32) at temperature $$T$$. The pressure of the gas is $$p$$. An identical vessel containing one mole of He gas (molar mass 4) at temperature $$4 T$$ has a pressure of
explanation:
To determine the pressure of the helium (He) gas in the identical vessel, we can use the ideal gas law:

$$PV = nRT$$

Given that both vessels are identical, they have the same volume, $$V$$. Also, each vessel contains one mole of gas, so $$n = 1$$ for both cases. Let's first express the pressure $$p$$ of the oxygen (O2) gas in terms of the given conditions:

For the oxygen gas:

$$pV = nRT$$

Substituting the given values:

$$pV = (1 \text{ mole}) \cdot R \cdot T$$

Therefore,

$$pV = RT$$

Now, consider the helium gas in the other vessel at temperature $$4T$$. According to the ideal gas law:

$$P_{He}V = nRT_{He}$$

Here, $${T_{He} = 4T}$$. Substituting the known values:

$$P_{He}V = (1 \text{ mole}) \cdot R \cdot (4T)$$

Therefore,

$$P_{He}V = 4RT$$

Since $$pV = RT$$ from our earlier equation for the oxygen gas, we can substitute $$RT$$ with $$pV$$ in the helium pressure equation:

$$P_{He}V = 4(pV)$$

Dividing both sides by $$V$$ to isolate $$P_{He}$$:

$$P_{He} = 4p$$

Therefore, the pressure of the helium gas at temperature $$4T$$ is $$4p$$.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option C: 4p

content: A Carnot's heat engine works between the temperature $$527^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ and $$127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$. What amount of heat should it consume per second to deliver mechanical work at the rate of $$1.0 \mathrm{~kW}$$ ?
explanation:
A Carnot heat engine operates between two temperatures: the hot reservoir temperature ($ T_h $) and the cold reservoir temperature ($ T_c $). To find the efficiency ($ \eta $) of a Carnot engine, we use the formula:

$$ \eta = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h} $$

where both temperatures ($ T_h $ and $ T_c $) must be in absolute temperature, i.e., Kelvin (K).

The given temperatures are $ 527^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $ and $ 127^{\circ} \mathrm{C} $. First, we convert these temperatures to Kelvin:

$$ T_h = 527^{\circ} \mathrm{C} + 273 = 800 \, \mathrm{K} $$

$$ T_c = 127^{\circ} \mathrm{C} + 273 = 400 \, \mathrm{K} $$

Next, we calculate the efficiency of the Carnot engine:

$$ \eta = 1 - \frac{400}{800} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 $$

The efficiency ($ \eta $) is equal to the fraction of heat converted to work. Therefore, the fraction of heat used to do mechanical work per second is:

$$ \eta = \frac{W}{Q_h} $$

where $ W $ is the work done per second (which is $ 1.0 \, \text{kW} $ or $ 1000 \, \text{W} $), and $ Q_h $ is the heat consumed per second.

Rearranging the formula, we get:

$$ Q_h = \frac{W}{\eta} $$

Substituting the given values:

$$ Q_h = \frac{1000 \, \mathrm{W}}{0.5} = 2000 \, \mathrm{W} $$

To convert the heat consumed per second from Watts to kcal/s, we use the conversion factor $ 1 \, \mathrm{kcal/s} = 4184 \, \mathrm{W} $:

$$ Q_h = \frac{2000 \, \mathrm{W}}{4184 \, \mathrm{W/kcal}} \approx 0.477 \, \mathrm{kcal/s} $$

Therefore, the amount of heat the Carnot engine should consume per second to deliver mechanical work at the rate of $ 1.0 \, \text{kW} $ is:

Option A: 0.477 kcal/s

content: A monoatomic gas of molar mass m is kept in a insulated container. Container is moving with velocity v. If the container is suddenly stopped, then the change in the temperature of the gas is
explanation:
Let the insulated container has n moles of the monoatomic gas. The loss of kinetic energy of the gas,

$$\Delta {E_K} = {1 \over 2}(mn){v^2}$$

If the change in temperature of the gas is $$\Delta$$T, then heat gained by the gas is,

$$\Delta Q = {3 \over 2}nR\,\Delta T$$

Now, according to question,

$$\Delta Q = \Delta {E_K}$$

$${3 \over 2}nR\,\Delta T = {1 \over 2}mn{v^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \Delta T = {{m{v^2}} \over {3R}}$$

content: One mole of an ideal diatomic gas undergoes a process as shown in the figure. The molar specific heat of the gas in the process is
explanation:
In the curve, V $$\propto$$ $$\frac{1}{T}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ V = m$$\frac{1}{T}$$ where, m = slope of curve.

$$\Rightarrow$$ VT = constant

$$\therefore$$ TdV + VdT = 0 ..... (i)

$$\Rightarrow$$ dV = $$-\frac{V}{T}$$dT

As, Q = $$\Delta$$U + W

CdT = CVdT + pdV ..... (ii)

CdT = CVdT $$-$$ $$\frac{p}{T}$$ VdT

$$\Rightarrow$$ C = CV $$-$$ $$\frac{pV}{T}$$

= CV $$-$$ R ($$\because$$ $$\frac{pV}{T}$$ = R)

For diatomic gas, CV = $$\frac{5}{2}$$R

$$\therefore$$ C = CV $$-$$ R = $$\frac{5}{2}$$R $$-$$ R = $$\frac{3R}{2}$$

content:
An ideal monoatomic gas is taken round the cycle ABCDA as shown in the p-diagram

The work done during the cycle is
explanation:
Work done in the cyclic process

= Area of the loop ABCD

= (2p $$-$$ p) $$\times$$ (2V $$-$$ V) = pV

content: Which of the following graphs show the correct relation between conductivity and temperature for a metallic conductor?
explanation:
Conductivity ($$\sigma$$) of a metallic conductor is given by

$$\sigma = {1 \over \rho } = {{n{e^2}\tau } \over m}$$

where, $$\rho$$ = resistivity of conductor,

$$\tau$$ = relaxation time,

n = number of free electrons

and m = mass.

For metals, as the temperature increases, the relaxation time $$\tau$$ decreases because collisions become more frequent. Hence, the conductivity of a metallic conductor decreases with the increases of temperature.

content: The ratio of the specific heats $${{{C_v}} \over {{C_p}}} = {1 \over \gamma }$$ in terms of degrees of freedom (n) is given by
explanation:
As we know,

$${C_v} = {n \over 2}R$$ ...... (i)

$${C_p} = \left( {{n \over 2} + 1} \right)R$$ ..... (ii)

On dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get

$${{{C_p}} \over {{C_v}}} = \gamma = {{\left( {{n \over 2} + 1} \right)} \over {{n \over 2}}} = 1 + {2 \over n}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{C_p}} \over {{C_v}}} = 1 + {2 \over n}$$

content: A black body radiates energy at the rate E Wm$$-$$2 at high temperature TK. When the temperature is reduced to $$\left( {{T \over 4}} \right)$$ K, the new radiant energy is
explanation:
Let E' be the new radiant energy.

According to Stefan's law,

E $$\propto$$ T4

$$\Rightarrow$$ $${{{E_2}} \over {{E_1}}} = {\left( {{{{T_2}} \over {{T_1}}}} \right)^4}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ $${{E'} \over E} = {\left\{ {{{(T/4)} \over T}} \right\}^4} = {\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^4}$$

or, $$E' = {E \over {256}}$$

content: In an adiabatic process, where pressure is decreased by $${3 \over 4}$$%, if $${{{C_p}} \over {{C_v}}} = {4 \over 3}$$, then the volume increases by
explanation:
In adiabatic process,

pV$$\gamma$$ = K

where, K is a constant.

Given that, $$\gamma = {4 \over 3} \Rightarrow p{V^{4/3}} = K$$

Taking logarithm on both sides, we get

$$\log p + {4 \over 3}\log V = \log K$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\Delta p} \over p} + {4 \over 3}{{\Delta V} \over V} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\Delta V} \over V} = - {3 \over 4}{{\Delta p} \over p}$$

Now, % change in volume,

$${{\Delta V} \over V} \times 100 = - {3 \over 4}\left( {{{\Delta p} \over p} \times 100} \right)$$

It is given that, pressure decreases by $${3 \over 4}$$%,

i.e. $${{\Delta p} \over p} \times 100 = - {3 \over 4}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{\Delta V} \over V} \times 100 = - {3 \over 4} \times \left( { - {3 \over 4}} \right) = {9 \over {16}}$$%

Hence, the volume increases by $${9 \over {16}}$$%.

content: A Carnot engine has the same efficiency between 600 K to 300 K and 1600 K to x K, then the value of x is
explanation:
Efficiency of Carnot engine is given by

$$\eta = 1 - {{{T_2}} \over {{T_1}}}$$

Given, T1 = 600 K

T2 = 300 K

T'1 = 1600 K and T'2 = x K

Carnot engine has same efficiency between 600 K to 300 K and 1600 K to x K, so

$$\left( {1 - {{{T_2}} \over {{T_1}}}} \right) = \left( {1 - {{T{'_2}} \over {T{'_1}}}} \right)$$

Substituting all the given values in above equation, we get

$$1 - {{300} \over {600}} = 1 - {x \over {1600}}$$

$$1 - {1 \over 2} = 1 - {x \over {1600}}$$

$${x \over {1600}} = 1 - {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{1600} \over 2} = 800$$ K

content: A vessel contains one mole of O2 gas (molar mass 32) at a temperature T. The pressure of the gas is p. An identical vessel containing one mole of He gas (molar mass 4) at a temperature 2T has a pressure of
explanation:
From gas equation,

$$pV = nRT \Rightarrow p = {{nRT} \over V}$$

Pressure of one mole of O2 gas,

$${(p)_{{O_2}}} = {{1 \times RT} \over V}$$ ..... (i)

Pressure of one mole of He gas,

$${(p)_{He}} = {{1 \times R(2T)} \over V}$$ ...... (ii)

Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get

$$\therefore$$ $${{{{(p)}_{He}}} \over {{{(p)}_{{O_2}}}}} = 2 \Rightarrow {(p)_{He}} = 2{(p)_{{O_2}}} = 2p$$

content: The figure shows graphs of pressure (p) versus density (d) for an ideal gas at two temperatures T1 and T2, then
explanation:
From Ideal gas equation,

$$pV = nnT = {{mRT} \over M}$$

$$n = {{Molar\,mass\,(m)} \over {Molecular\,weight\,(M)}}$$

Here, n = number of moles, R = Gas constant, V = volume

$$ \Rightarrow {{pV} \over m} = {{RT} \over M}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {p \over {(m/V)}} = {{RT} \over M}$$

$${p \over d} = {{RT} \over m}$$ [$$\because$$ $${m \over v} = d$$]

$$ \Rightarrow {p \over d} \propto T$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ Slope of p versus d graph $$\propto$$ T

Since, slope is less for the gas at temperature T1.

Therefore, T1 < T2

content: When 2 moles of a monoatomic gas are mixed with 3 moles of a diatomic gas, the value of adiabatic exponent for the mixture is
explanation:
Let, $$\gamma$$ be the adiabatic exponent for the mixture.

For monoatomic gas,

n1 = 2 and $$\gamma$$1 = $${{5} \over {3}}$$

For diatomic gas,

n2 = 3 and $$\gamma$$2 = $${{7} \over {5}}$$

For the mixture,

$${{{n_1} + {n_2}} \over {\gamma - 1}} = {{{n_1}} \over {{\gamma _1} - 1}} + {{{n_2}} \over {{\gamma _2} - 1}}$$

Substituting all the given values in above equation, we get

$${{2 + 3} \over {\gamma - 1}} = {2 \over {{5 \over 3} - 1}} + {3 \over {{7 \over 5} - 1}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {5 \over {\gamma - 1}} = {{21} \over 2} \Rightarrow \gamma = {{31} \over {21}}$$
